run.output:
Q1 result count: 4 rows
Execution time: 771.68 ms

validation.comparison_result: Q1 results match ground truth (4 rows expected, 4 rows actual)

Q1.notes: Correctness validated. Performance is 16x slower than target (48ms baseline) and 8.9x slower than DuckDB (87ms). Code has parallelism and SIMD but missing key optimizations.

Q1.bottleneck: I/O bound on HDD with missing zone map optimization and suboptimal memory access patterns
Q1.root_cause: The code is scanning all 60M rows (~1.7GB of data) without using the existing zone map index. Even though selectivity is 98.8% (zone maps won't skip much), the code doesn't try. More critically: (1) unaligned SIMD loads add ~10-20% overhead, (2) missing MADV_WILLNEED prefetching on HDD causes cache misses, (3) scattered memory access pattern across 7 columns creates poor spatial locality on HDD, (4) perfect hash uses multiplication instead of bit shifts.

analysis.overall: Q1 iteration 1 produces correct results but is 16x slower than the 48ms baseline. The code has good foundational optimizations (64-thread parallelism, AVX-512 SIMD, morsel-driven execution, thread-local aggregation) but leaves significant performance on the table. Primary bottlenecks: (1) unused zone map index despite existing file, (2) suboptimal memory access patterns on HDD (unaligned loads, no prefetching), (3) inefficient perfect hash computation, (4) potential for algorithmic improvements (filter-first approach to reduce data movement). On HDD with ~150MB/s bandwidth and 1.7GB to read, theoretical minimum is ~11 seconds, but we're getting 771ms due to warm page cache. This suggests the bottleneck is CPU-side (memory access patterns, cache misses) rather than raw I/O bandwidth. Hardware: 64 cores (AVX-512), 376GB RAM, 44MB L3 cache, HDD storage.

performance_optimizations.description: Replace unaligned SIMD loads (_mm512_loadu_si512) with aligned loads (_mm512_load_si512) at line 272. Verify column data is 64-byte aligned in storage format. Aligned loads provide ~15-25% better throughput on AVX-512. If storage is unaligned, keep unaligned loads but add explicit prefetching with _mm_prefetch() for next cache line to hide latency on HDD.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-20% speedup (77-154ms reduction). Aligned loads eliminate alignment penalties and improve memory bandwidth utilization. On AVX-512, unaligned loads incur extra cycle penalties when crossing cache line boundaries.

performance_optimizations.description: Add MADV_WILLNEED hints in MMapColumn::open() for HDD workloads (lines 120-143). Before starting parallel scan, issue madvise(MADV_WILLNEED) for all column files to trigger kernel readahead. On HDD, random access patterns from 64 threads competing can cause seek storms. Prefetching entire columns sequentially at startup amortizes seeks. For Q1 with 1.7GB working set and 376GB RAM, everything fits in page cache after first touch.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 15-30% speedup on cold cache (116-231ms reduction first run). Minimal impact on warm cache. Transforms random HDD seeks into sequential reads by coordinating kernel I/O scheduling.

performance_optimizations.technique: Bit-Shift Perfect Hash Instead of Multiplication
performance_optimizations.description: Replace perfect hash computation 'l_returnflag * 2 + l_linestatus' with bit operations at lines 284 and 316. Since returnflag and linestatus are both single-byte values (0-2 and 0-1), use '(l_returnflag << 1) | l_linestatus' instead. Bit shifts and OR are 1-cycle ops vs 3-4 cycles for integer multiply on modern CPUs. This is in the hot path (executed 60M times).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-8% speedup (39-62ms reduction). Small per-operation savings multiply across 60M rows. Modern CPUs have low-latency multipliers, so gain is modest but measurable in tight loops.

performance_optimizations.description: Current implementation extracts each matching row from SIMD mask and processes scalar (lines 279-307). Instead, use SIMD horizontal operations to accumulate partial sums directly in vector registers. Use _mm512_mask_add_epi32 for qty accumulation, _mm512_mask_add_epi64 for price, and reduce to scalar only at morsel boundaries. This eliminates the scalar loop inside the SIMD batch processing. Requires restructuring aggregation to hold SIMD accumulators.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 15-25% speedup (116-193ms reduction). Reduces instruction count by staying in SIMD domain longer. However, implementation complexity is higher due to perfect hash (can't vectorize hash table updates easily). May need to switch to scalar hash after SIMD filtering.

performance_optimizations.technique: Two-Phase Filtering (Date Filter First, Then Load Other Columns)
performance_optimizations.description: Current approach loads all 7 columns upfront. With 98.8% selectivity on l_shipdate, we're loading ~1.2% unnecessary data for the other 6 columns. Restructure to: (1) SIMD scan l_shipdate and produce selection vector, (2) gather only matching row positions, (3) load other 6 columns only for selected rows using scattered loads. For 1.2% rejection, this saves ~20MB of data movement (1.2% of 1.7GB). On HDD, scatter-gather overhead likely exceeds benefit at this selectivity. More effective if selectivity drops (e.g., Q6 at 2.4% selectivity).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Minimal at 98.8% selectivity (<2%, ~15ms). Would be 20-40% at lower selectivities like Q6 (2.4%). Not recommended for Q1 specifically, but useful pattern for other queries.

performance_optimizations.technique: Zone Map Integration (Even If Minimal Skipping)
performance_optimizations.description: Implement zone map loading and block skipping at lines 231-234 where TODO comment exists. The zone map file 'lineitem.l_shipdate.zonemap.idx' exists (15KB, ~600 blocks of 100K rows). For cutoff_date = 1998-09-02 with 98.8% selectivity, zone maps will skip ~1.2% of blocks (data is sorted by l_shipdate per storage design). Format appears to be array of (min_val, max_val) int32 pairs. Load zone map, iterate blocks, skip if block.min_val > cutoff_date. Even with minimal skipping, this demonstrates proper index usage for future queries.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Minimal for Q1 (~1-2%, 8-15ms) due to high selectivity. Architectural improvement for consistency and future queries with better selectivity. Fixes the TODO and makes the system complete.

performance_optimizations.technique: Reduce Morsel Size for Better Cache Locality
performance_optimizations.description: Current morsel size is 50K rows. With 7 columns and 64 threads, working set per thread per morsel is ~50K * (4+4+8+4+4+1+1) = 1.3MB, which exceeds L2 cache (1.28MB = 32MB/32 cores) but fits in L3 (44MB/32 cores = 1.4MB per core). Reduce to 25K-30K rows to fit entirely in L2 cache, improving temporal locality and reducing L3/DRAM traffic. L2 hit latency is ~12 cycles vs ~40 cycles for L3.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-12% speedup (39-93ms reduction). Better cache utilization reduces memory stalls. Trade-off: more morsel boundary overhead (aggregation merges), but at 64 threads the parallelism overhead is already amortized.

performance_optimizations.technique: Array-Based Aggregation Instead of Perfect Hash
performance_optimizations.description: Current implementation uses perfect hash 'returnflag*2+linestatus' to index into array (line 284). This works but multiplies unnecessarily. Since there are only 6 possible groups (3 returnflags × 2 linestatuses), directly use a 6-element array indexed by (rf << 1) | ls. This is already partially done (local_agg is std::array<AggValue, 6>), but the indexing expression can be optimized further. More impactfully, consider if returnflag/linestatus are already sequential 0-2 and 0-1 from dictionary encoding. If so, the bit-shift optimization in priority 3 covers this.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Covered by priority 3 optimization (bit-shift perfect hash). Redundant as separate item.

performance_optimizations.description: Code uses Kahan summation for sum_disc_price and sum_charge (lines 82-95) to maintain numerical accuracy. For TPC-H scale factor 10 with ~60M rows, standard double precision (53-bit mantissa) loses ~3-4 digits of precision without compensation. Kahan summation adds 5 extra FP operations per accumulation. If accuracy requirements allow (verify with benchmark comparison tool), switch to simple += operator. Alternatively, use pairwise summation (hierarchical reduction) which has better accuracy than naive summation with less overhead than Kahan.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 3-7% speedup (23-54ms reduction). Kahan summation is in the critical path (60M invocations). However, removing it may cause validation failures if ground truth expects high precision. Test carefully.

summary: Q1 iteration 1 is correct but 16x slower than baseline (771ms vs 48ms target). Code has strong foundation with 64-thread parallelism, AVX-512 SIMD, and thread-local aggregation. Key opportunities: (1) aligned SIMD loads for 10-20% gain [low risk], (2) MADV_WILLNEED prefetching on HDD for 15-30% gain on cold cache [low risk], (3) bit-shift perfect hash for 5-8% gain [low risk], (4) SIMD horizontal aggregation for 15-25% gain [medium risk], (5) reduced morsel size for better L2 cache fit (5-12% gain) [low risk]. Zone map integration provides minimal benefit at 98.8% selectivity but fixes architectural gap. Combined, low-risk optimizations (priorities 1-3, 7) could reduce time to ~500-550ms (35% improvement). Adding medium-risk SIMD horizontal aggregation could reach ~400-450ms (45-48% improvement). Recommend starting with priorities 1-3 (aligned loads, prefetching, bit-shift hash) as they are low-risk, well-understood, and cumulatively provide ~30-40% speedup.
